full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right, everybody, welcome back to This Week in Startups. I'm your host, Jason Calacanis. As you know, we have a program called Founder University. Founder University is a 12-week course for people in year zero of startups, people who apply for funding from our venture firm but are too soon for funding. So we said, wow, all this incredible energy, everybody emailing us, applying for funding, literally over 10,000 people a year. How do we capture that energy and help founders solve the world's biggest problems, or solve the problems that are important to them, maybe you as consumers. So we created this 12-week course where we go over all the basics. How to set up a cap table, how to get product-market fit, how to do go-to-market strategies, how to find a co-founder, how to present your product, how to understand your total addressable market, maybe find your ideal customer profile, just design, UX, all of it. The founders typically understand about half of what we teach them, but for different founders that 50% is different. Some of them are awesome at operations but their design is terrible, but they're really good at go-to-market, or they're absolutely extraordinary at recruiting talent but they don't even know what the term ICP or customer acquisition cost, they're not familiar with those, and that's okay because everybody becomes familiar with these techniques and these strategies over time. So we meet the founders where they are, and my team spends time with them. It's incredibly rewarding, it's...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Incredibly hard. Most of the startups fail. But they come back a second or third time and then sometimes even on the first shot they make it. And it's one of the great joys in my life to take Founder University from the United States to my favorite country in the world after the United States. And maybe don't tell anybody but maybe I like it a little more than the United States. And that's Japan. And it's been the thrill of my life to have the Japanese government JETRO ask me to bring Founder University here. And we're doing our first cohort. We've had so many of my friends over the years of coming here for 30 years say yes to coming and mentoring the founders here. 30 founders joined the first cohort. It's a very competitive process to get in. If you want to learn more about it just go to founder.university. You can apply to do it in the United States, you can apply to do it in Riyadh in Saudi Arabia, in the kingdom, we do it there with Sanabil. You can apply to do it here in Japan. But please, no more countries. I'm saying this with peace and love. I'm doing a lot of traveling. Okay, maybe one more country, but that's it. After that, I'm going to say no. Maybe we'll do one more, I don't know. So here we are at Founder University. It's the end of the program, of the first week of the program. It's the third day and we had an intense couple of days, but I'm really excited because Shin Takamiya, I met you 20 years ago.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: 20, 15 years ago? Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: 25 years ago? I don't know.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Maybe not that back, 20. 20, yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: We're very old now, you know Shin? You and I are old.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And we learned some things. So today we'll talk about what we've learned and how things have changed. I was talking earlier in the program, I got invited to come speak and back then I would maybe get invited once in a while, maybe they'd pay for my flight. Big deal for me back then because I was broke. I had no money. So to get a free coach flight and fly here to me was like the dream. And you and I met at a conference. I had sold Weblogs Inc, was doing Engadget, and I would always go to Akihabara, the electronics here, and I had seen VR and really augmented reality for the first time.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: You remember this back 20 years ago? And already Japan was coming up with the VR figure and Jason was so excited about it and he was just hanging around this Otaku anime kind of figure.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What happened was I was walking through Akihabara and I would go there with my writers from Engadget and they would take me to all the different buildings. In the buildings there would be 10 stories. So we'd go to the 10th floor, there'd be robots, like little miniature robots. Then the next floor would be robot cars. Then the next floor would be robots that went in water. Each of the different floors. And then I saw this big line with a bunch of salarymen in line after work in their suits. They looked pretty tired and they were online to buy something. So I went to the front of the line and they were buying an action figure in a box of software. And you would take the webcam off the computer, webcams were pretty new, they didn't work very well. And then they had a paddle, like a stick with a QR code and a little box. You put the box down, you have your PC...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: See, and you take the image and you put it on the box, and pop out of the box would be a little character. And then you would interact with the character. And I said, whoa, that's could be the future. It was a little too early.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: A little bit.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah. But they were buying it and this is one of the great things about Japanese culture. You're always five, 10, 20 years ahead of everybody else. What do you attribute it to? The creativity and everything. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: I mean, the VR figure back then was already quite advanced in Japan, but you know, if you look back, you know, you could say we were pretty advanced. And even from a Japanese standpoint, it wasn't a mainstream. It was like a, you know, kind of otaku culture. But now otaku culture is everywhere in the world, like you see anime—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What is otaku culture?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Otaku culture is like a Japanese pop culture kind of rooted around anime and manga and things like that. Initially, maybe 30, 40 years back, it was regarded as a kind of geeky hobby.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: The worst geeky hobby.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and otaku was kind of, sometimes it was like a some kind of, you know, bad word to say if you were told you're otaku.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Like a nerd. Or a geek. Dweeb.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah. But now, you know—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I'm not calling you that.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: I am otaku, so it's okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, but now you're cool. Otaku became cool.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah. Exactly, exactly. And, you know, otaku is highly related to IP business. So, you know, it's definitely something we can export out of Japan.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, IP, intellectual property, became, I think, a very, very powerful concept here. And I remember in 1992, I was working for Sony, and they became, the Sony Corporation said, hey, if we can do convergence, we can bring together consumer electronics, music—so they bought Columbia Records—and movies, we buy Paramount, and then we would do convergence. We were even doing radio. And that was when I first got exposed to the concept of, hey, all of these things are going to come together. And here we are. It's come together.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Back then, Idei-san came up with, the CEO Idei-san came up with the concept Digital Media Kids, which is DMK.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: A yes! DMK, Digital Media Kids. Yeah, that was so cool. On a floppy disk, and they would say, here is everything you need to understand the topic. A text file, some pictures, short video clips. All of that then became deliverable on the internet, online services. It's fascinating. So Shin, you work for Globis Capital Partners. This is one of the largest venture firms here.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And so today we're going to talk about your investing here and then just some observations about the global market. Maybe tell us a little bit about what you do, how long you've been a venture capitalist.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Okay. So we Globis Capital Partners are one of the biggest VC firms here, and we are actually one of the oldest VC firms here. So we started out in 1996, back then there was no IP.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Growth market for tech companies. So, Nasdaq Japan and the Mothers market of Tokyo Stock Exchange only came in in the early 2000s. You know, we kind of grew together with the Japanese startup ecosystem. So we're kind of like brothers to the Japanese startup ecosystem. We were a startup incidentally doing VC business back then. And as for myself, I spent like six years of my childhood in Europe and went to US to get my MBA.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Where'd you go?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: MBA?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Harvard.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Oh, okay. It's okay, right? I've never heard of it. Where's it from? It's somewhere cold. Boston, I think. Somewhere Cambridge.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: You can always know if somebody went to Harvard, because they never mention it. They say, I got an MBA. And then I say where? And they say, oh, Massachusetts. And then I say, oh, where in Massachusetts? They say, Boston. I say, oh, where in Boston? They say, Cambridge. It's because if you say you went to Harvard, everybody says, oh, too smart or whatever. But you went to HBS.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And you did that in the 90s, late 90s?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: No, no, I was class of '08. 2008, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: 2008. Okay, you went later. And when did you start in venture capital? And why did you decide venture capital?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: So I joined Globis right after my MBA, so it was 2008. That was like right after the subprime bubble burst.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes, we had the Great Recession in America. Everybody lost their jobs. The Nasdaq went from 5,000 to 1,500, and everybody thought it was the end of the technology business.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: But it was actually good timing for you. Exactly. Enter low and exit high, you know.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It is, paradoxically, the best time to get in venture capital is at the down market.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yep, yep, exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: If you're an independent content creator, or you're building any kind of community-oriented business like I am, just making great videos or awesome websites, it's not enough. You need to spend real time with your community. And you need to do that with tools that allow you to see what's going on and communicate smoothly and crisply with your community members. That's why I'm so happy to partner with Circle, the complete community platform for creators and brands. With Circle, you're going to get an easy way to build branded websites that help you with email marketing strategies. That's super clutch. And AI agents that can help you design, manage data, and even do the branding. We use Circle here at Launch to keep up with our Founder University community. We've done 14 Founder Universities around the world, and they've helped us grow that from this tiny little program out of our office in Austin to this bona fide international movement. And they can do the same for your startup. Twist listeners can get $1,000 off Circle's Professional Plan by going to circle.so/twist. That's C-I-R-C-L-E.so/twist.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What stage do you invest in typically?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: So currently we

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: We typically stop from pre-A or A kind of stage.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay, so seed and series A.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Pre-A kind of... yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Oh, okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: So we would like to see a team and the product. You don't have to have a massive traction, but enough traction so that we can get a glimpse of PMF. So that's like the initial stages we would invest, but we would follow on anything into the late stage. So we say we are the first and first to last. So we are the first institutional VC to back a founder and the last we would back it to the last round before IPO.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay, so full life cycle basically. And that means the companies here are a little bit too early. So when he finishes, don't rush the stage. You can meet him, but the likelihood of investing in a year zero startup or pre-product market fit is too early for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: A little bit. But probably I should know you guys now because you know, you know it's a very kind of relation business. You don't want to invest into somebody you don't know, you don't trust. You know typically when I invest I like to invest into my friends who I know for like over two years or something like that. So I can trust the person and I know that person from like everyday life rather than knowing him under cosmetics when he's fundraising.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And this is an important lesson for founders is to play the long game. You're going to meet people, you want them to understand what you're building and why you're building it. And then they can put a little checkmark in their journal, in their database. 'Oh okay, I met this founder, they're doing something interesting, yeah'.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: So even in series A, you know, I and maybe we believe that the founder is the single most important factor in investing and especially their motivation. You know, we don't want to invest into people who's interest into money or power. You know, we look for some, you know, truly rooted down motivation why they want to do this business. They would have like a passion for it or they would have like almost like a karma to do that business or something like that. So we want to know why the founder is doing that business and if that why is solid, you know, you can, you know, you would have the grit to continue the business when you're even in the hard things. And in the end, if the founder doesn't give up, the business is still alive, so you know, you always need that strong motivation or driver why the founder is doing the business.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: This is really important for founders to understand that our number one fear, our nightmare as investors is not that the company runs out of money or they have to pivot, it's that the founder gives up.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Exactly. So we don't want founders, you know, just fleeing irresponsibly. So, you know, and of course, you know, sometimes it's important for the founders to rightly give up because after all, if you look at it from...

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: From the macro perspective, it's a kind of utility business as I look at it. So the founder is the most precious resource in the startup community and you want to maximize the utility of the founder.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes. So if the founder picks an idea, they have the karma, they're passionate for it, and they get two, three years into this adventure and they realize the market isn't ready, the market doesn't want it, they can't force consumers, they can't force a business to want their vision of the world. So, yeah, sometimes it's the right thing to shut it down.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah. So, you know, it's okay that your hypothesis is wrong. You know, it, you know, what is not okay is you just kind of, you know, be irresponsible or, you know, you do some kind of immoral things or things like that. But, you know, if your hypothesis does make sense, but it doesn't turn out to be right, I think it's a nice try. And even from our investor kind of perspective, we'd like to invest into those kind of founders. You know, it's not the result that which matters. So if founders had a very logical hypothesis or investment theme that made sense but if he fails, it's okay. We want to invest again into that serial entrepreneur.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: This is also very important. If failure in the United States, we champion it. We are excited to see—it's not what we want to happen—but when we see a founder fail, it's almost universally, if they don't give up, the precursor to later success. In Japan, the culture, though, of failure comes with shame?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Used to.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It used to. When did it change and why?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Um, I think it changed in the last 10 years. Just to throw out a showcase number, when I joined Globis as a venture capitalist, there was only like $300 million investment per year in all of Japan. But nowadays we have like $10 billion USD. So, you know, it's grown so fast. And one of my portfolio companies, Mercari, was the very first Japanese unicorn to go public, which went public in 2018. And only after seven years, we had—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Mercari?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Mercari, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: M-E-R-C-A-R-I? Yes. It's a marketplace.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yes, exactly. It's a marketplace.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Like Craigslist or eBay? How would Americans think of it?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Uh, it's the equivalent is Poshmark.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Oh, Poshmark, yeah. So designer clothes or designed products?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Initially started out from kind of designer clothes for ladies and for kids because they—it's the same kind of seller and there was a synergy between different categories. But as they grew, uh, they are dominant kind of second-hand app in Japan. So they kind of made—went into multi-categories and they're basically all category right now. But the point I wanted to make was only after seven years of the founding—

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: first unicorn going public, we had 77 startups that was valued over 1 billion.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Wow.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: The caveat is that includes not only unicorns but also includes those companies that touch down to the 1 billion dollar market cap line after one year of going public.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: So, one point I wanted to make is this is how vibrant Japanese startup community is and how much younger generation is becoming entrepreneurs and you know startups are becoming cool, so the traditional kind of stereotype image of Japanese wearing suits being what we call salarymen,

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: you know, lifetime employment, that's totally gone right now.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Totally gone for young people.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: For young people.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: They still have it as an option.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yes. But there now it's number two on the list, number three on the list of possibilities.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, maybe.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: So another kind of a showcase, it's like a joke but I always tell, number one job sought after McKinsey is now startups.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: McKinsey used to be number one?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Uh, you know, it's one of the prestige you know jobs you can get and you know typically people might go join a big Japanese blue-chip company to become one of the you know top management or something like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: That was high status.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yes. And now high status has shifted for young people to being a founder or even joining...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: ...or associated with startups. Yes, exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: This is very interesting for people to understand coming here it's less transactional, it's more character based. Honor, karma, it's cool to be with startups. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Shin, you're at Globis Capital Partners, $500 million fund, new fund coming, you invest series A sometimes a little before and in the last round before they go public. We'll talk about going public, but for the founders here, what should this ideal meeting look like?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And what is the protocol here in Japan for getting that meeting and what is expected to occur in that meeting?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: I don't think there's much difference between, you know, having a investor meeting in the US. So, to answer your first question about what the founders should do with the first meeting, so to be very tactical or like a hacker mind, I think the whole objective of the first meeting is to get the investors interested. You don't have to tell all of the story, you just want to focus on one single value proposition you have and get that investor interested and then, you know, once you get that investor interested, you would naturally have a follow-up meeting or follow-up questions. So single important thing is to, you know, get his interest. That's the only thing you need.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Right. You want them to understand what you're doing, understand why you're doing it, understand why you're the right person to do it. And then you want to get them to ask questions, yeah? Yes. And so what is the best way—sometimes founders get a little nervous venture capitalists, oh, they have the money, they anoint me. But you and I know, especially my time as an entrepreneur, I'm not sure did you spend time as an entrepreneur as well or?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Um, not really, not really.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: As an entrepreneur, when you're first starting out, it's very intimidating and then later on you look at venture capitalists, okay, they support my company, but I still have to run it. So I could use their capital, I could use their advice on the margins. So maybe you're not as enamored with it. What's the way to answer the questions when you ask, you know, a very basic question, how should the founder respond to it, especially if they're a little nervous?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Probably the very basic is, I think you should try—you're not the person being judged, you should also judge the investor. After all, especially for the lead investor, you're going to be stuck with that person for like seven years or 10 years or even longer. Right. So you want to select the investor who you can get along with, who you have the right chemistry with. So you should take off your mind that you're being judged, you're also judging your opponent.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: And the game of getting the lead investor is not like the popularity kind of, you know, voting. You have to—the game is to find only one lead investor, the right investor. So, you know, if even 99 potential lead investors says no, that's okay, if you get one yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Right. It is a numbers game and that I think is very hard for a founder to handle early on. Rejection and a no is the default.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Right and, you know, when you get the rejection, you shouldn't feel that you or your baby is, you know...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Ugly.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya

full_transcript.text: What are you seeing? How are founders using AI to build companies faster, better? Do you have insights into it?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yeah, definitely, you know, AI has to be integrated into your business model. But again, it's about predicting the time. So what I always like to think when I'm thinking of my investment thesis or even to advise the founders or my portfolio companies.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Is that look at the advanced use cases. So AI is a big word, but there are some advanced use cases, let's say for example, autonomous cars, the tech is already ready, you know. Initially, people was in the main driver's seat, AI was just an assisting kind of tool.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Then the role of AI became stronger and you know, people just sat on the driver's seat to take responsibility because of the readiness of the society and the legal framework wasn't there. And finally, tech and the society is both ready, so there is full autonomous driving. And you know, autonomous driving was use kind of one of the advanced use cases, you know, you already have Waymo and all that already, you know, put into use. But if you look at other application, even like a enterprise solution or something like that, you hardly see any autonomous kind of fully agentic product yet.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: So the way I see it is in analogy to the autonomous driving is that yes, the tech is there, it's possible to build a product, but let's say if it's an enterprise product, your client might be very old, especially in Japan, the big Japanese traditional companies may be very conservative and they don't want to kind of implement fully blown like a autonomous AI agent. They would say like who is going to take the responsibility when it fails and things like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Risk is very important here.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: So that means your client, you know, in the analogy of autonomous driving, the society is not ready yet. So what's happening right now is there is like a human account manager and the quality assurance person who is just being there just for the sake of—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Human in the loop, monitoring.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, monitoring, so that the client can feel safe and you know, and the AI agentic kind of product is implemented within the workflow of the provider, not the client internally. So from the client perspective, it's just a cheaper and more efficient BPO, but internal process on the supplier side, it's fully AI. And that's like the you know, stage where there is like a human, you know, safety copilot is there. Maybe down the road, there's going to come like fully autonomous product where it would be implemented within the client's workflow. But you can't predict the time of readiness of the client side and you know, like regulatory issues like security or information things like that. So in the short term, you have to take what is available on the table with the short-term business model, but at the same time, it's pretty difficult to have a conflicting business model within your company, but it would dominate five years down the road and you don't know when the switching time would come.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Hmm. So you have to have both business model embedded.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And this is where the strategy comes in.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yes, yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: As a founder, you have to figure out how to survive. So if fully autonomous cars aren't here yet, but you can give it to a driver so they're less fatigued, which was the original idea that this will lower fatigue, maybe lower mistakes. Okay, great. Like adaptive cruise control. I'll keep you in the lane, and that's some value, not the full value.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: And it kind of helps you develop elemental product or elemental technology to feed into the, you know, fully autonomous driving in the end. So it kind of leads the way into the end goal.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Shin, it's been an amazing to spend an hour with you and catch up. Thank you so much for coming on This Week in Startups. And I'm going to be here twice a year, so I don't know what your favorite restaurant is or food, tell me now.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Actually, you know, there's tons of Japanese great restaurants, you know, there's some restaurant you can't reserve at head like two years.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Right. Which you're very important, and you know everybody.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: And you know, as we said, we live in a longer time horizon, so two years is like, you know, two weeks down the road.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What's your favorite food right now? What do you love?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Right now, maybe there's the restaurant or actually there's a good sushi restaurant in Tokyo, Kioicho.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Kioicho. Mitani.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Is it Omakase?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Omakase, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Michelin star or like more underground?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: It's not underground, but it doesn't have a star, but it's super famous. Some of the best restaurants in Japan, they don't have stars.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: They don't want them. Exactly. Yeah, because they want to be for their customers and their community, yeah?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Loyal customers with higher LTVs.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And actually thinking correctly, I found an interesting concept. I had heard that sushi used to be bar food.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: It was a fast food in Edo period, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah. And so having done this research many years ago on the history of it, they used to be stand-up sushi bars. And when I came here, I was talking to one of my founders and they said, oh yeah, we were going shopping and he said there's a stand-up sushi bar I like a lot in the basement of this department store. You should go to it. And I went and you stand up and it's all these incredible cuts, incredible otoro, incredible Hokkaido uni, just all the best pieces, but the price is like half of the normal restaurant in Japan. You get it faster and you stand there. And then there's a little hot spigot and you take a little matcha powder and you make your own green tea. No waitresses, just order from the chef, boom, boom, boom. And that is one-fourth of the price in the United States. It was so good. So I've been to two stand-up sushi places. Have you, you know about these?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Are they common or not common?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yes. Yeah, I know.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Pretty common. You actually have one in Haneda Airport too.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Haneda Airport has one?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Really?

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Yes. Now you know where to find Jason.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I'll be there.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Alright. Let's give it up one more time for Shin Takamiya.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Thank you.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Alright everybody. Uh, we'll have one more episode or two on This Week in Startups from Tokyo. And it has been what a thrill and an honor and a privilege to get to spend time with these founders. We'll see you all next time. Bye-bye.

full_transcript.speaker: Shin Takamiya
full_transcript.text: Take care.

highlights: The traditional Japanese "salaryman" culture and lifetime employment model are being replaced by a new generation that views startup entrepreneurship and founder roles as the ultimate high-status career path.
Japan’s startup ecosystem has experienced a massive transformation over the last decade, with annual venture capital investment growing from approximately $300 million to $10 billion.

Successful venture investing requires finding "founder-market fit," where the entrepreneur possesses a deep-rooted, non-monetary motivation or "karma" that provides the grit necessary to survive inevitable business challenges.

While AI technology is advancing faster than the internet did, widespread adoption currently requires a "human-in-the-loop" approach to manage risk and satisfy conservative clients until society and regulations are ready for fully autonomous agents.

Founders should view fundraising as a mutual selection process for a decade-long partnership, focusing on finding a single investor who aligns with their specific logical hypothesis rather than seeking universal approval.
summary:
This transcript features a conversation between Jason Calacanis and Shin Takamiya of Globis Capital Partners regarding the radical evolution of Japan’s startup ecosystem. They highlight a significant cultural shift where the traditional "salaryman" career path has been replaced by entrepreneurship as the high-status choice for the younger generation. Over the last decade, the Japanese venture capital market has matured significantly, growing from roughly $300 million to $10 billion in annual investment, accompanied by a newfound cultural acceptance of failure as a prerequisite for long-term success.

Takamiya outlines a character-based investment philosophy, emphasizing that a founder’s "karma," grit, and core motivation are more important than their initial product. He advises founders to treat the fundraising process as a long-term relationship-building exercise rather than a series of transactions, noting that investors look for a logical "thread of thinking" rather than just a perfect pitch. The discussion stresses that while the product may change, the founder’s ability to navigate volatility and maintain integrity remains the most critical factor for institutional backing.

The dialogue concludes with insights into scalable business models and the rapid emergence of artificial intelligence. Takamiya argues that successful ventures must target large, growing markets (TAM) and focus on business models where unit economics improve exponentially with scale. Regarding AI, he suggests that while the technology is ready, societal and regulatory "readiness" often lags behind. Consequently, he advises founders to adopt "human-in-the-loop" strategies to provide immediate value while preparing for a future dominated by fully autonomous AI agents.

Not long ago, promising young Japanese graduates wanted to go work for the largest, most established, and even oldest corporations: Sony, Mitsubishi, and the like. But now, just over the last few years, more and more Japanese people are becoming entrepreneurs and founders. 

TWiST Japan continues with a fascinating look inside the country’s growing startup ecosystem with special guest, venture capitalist Shinichi “Shin” Takamiya. 

He’ll walk Jason through how Japan stayed ahead of the rest of the world in technology, but started falling behind when it came to founding companies, and how the Japanese are now starting to level the playing field.
